speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three with survivor Howard Chandler. You had arrived in Auschwitz. You had gone through the delousing, and you had picked up the pajama-like uniforms. And now you're marching into the Zigeunerlager. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: When got to the gypsy camp, the Zigeunerlager, on one side, on the right side as we walked into the camp-- there were gypsies, I mean, German-speaking gypsies, families of gypsies. And we were assigned the left side of the camp. The camp must have been approximately a kilometer long. There were barracks on either side and a road in between. And past this, there were barbed wires and another camp like this and another camp like this. There were camps like this as far as your eye could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- on the way there, there was a Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]. She was a friend of my father's. And he got sick, and he had to be more assisted to-- to-- to-- to that camp. And I-- I still don't recall what happened with him. I don't know-- he-- he's no more alive. He got-- was taken sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got there, we were assembled in front of the barracks. We weren't allowed to go in to-- we were told to line up according alphabetical order. And we were given a tattoo, a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point. This was late in the afternoon already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were given a number as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was given a number. We lined up according to alphabetical order, like I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you show that to us now for a moment?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after this, we were given the rations. We were given the-- I think it was a piece of bread and a-- a bowl, what you would call, with some hot broth or soup. This was quite welcome. We haven't eaten for most of a week. And then we were assigned in the barrack. The barracks, as far as I can recall, I don't think had any bunks. So we were standing there. It was quite crowded, although you could find a place, maybe, where you could maybe squat down. And evening was approaching, and then you couldn't go outside of the barrack because it was curfew. After a certain time, I would suppose, that you-- no people were allowed outside of the barracks. So you had to stay in. But as the evening progressed and night came, and we could hear a lot of noises outside. You couldn't look out because the barrack didn't have windows. The windows were on top of the roof. And we could hear all kinds of noises, [INAUDIBLE] like heavy truck engines roaring, dogs barking. You could hear the old-- the odd shot being fired and shouts constantly, the dogs barking. And we didn't know what to make of it. And when we woke up in the morning, and we were allowed to go outside, there wasn't a single gypsy left. What happened that night is they backed the trucks up against the gates of the barracks, forced the gypsies on it, and in the morning, the crematoria were going full-blast. And this was the end of the gypsies in Auschwitz and Birkenau. We were made to clean the barracks, disinfect them. Then we were assigned these barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is summer of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- in-- in-- in July. I think it was July. It was very hot. I don't know what the date was exactly. It must have been late July. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a while, since we were considered a-- a good labor force, experienced ammunition workers, they used to pick groups of people, and they were sent away to wherever. They picked my brother together with another group. Made them go through this disinfection again, isolation or quarantine, and then they sent them away-- didn't know where. My father and I, with the rest of the people, I think, or most of the people, were transferred to the next adjoining camp, called D Camp. I was assigned a barrack together with my father. Then I was separated from my father to a different barrack because I worked in a different place where he worked. We were assigned work. I think at that time he worked in a place where they had a big warehouse where they used to bring in airplanes that are shot down for dismantling and salvaging of parts of the airplanes. And then we had different jobs. So my father and I didn't have-- since we didn't work on the same job, we were assigned different barracks. Then, I think it was late in November or-- that they picked my father together with another group. You get used to the life of the camp, and you could see what was taking place already, to be sent away somewhere. And I know that he was again quarantined in a-- in a-- in a barrack, and you couldn't get near there. But I knew eventually that they would have to come out of the camp, go through the outside of the perimeter of the camp, between which was a road leading to the railway, where we came in. That-- that's where they will lead them out, and I was waiting every day. And finally, I waited till they were taking them out. And I had already-- I was working already in the crematorias, taking out the clothing, taking them to a place called Kanada, where they had the warehouses full of the prisoners' belongings, like their shoes and socks, pants and suits and shaving brushes and eyeglasses and luggage and shaving pencils, [INAUDIBLE] you name it-- kitchen utensils and artificial limbs-- warehouses full of them. And these things were being sorted and sent away on a daily basis, I guess to Germany and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a sweater, an extra sweater I used. This was not allowed. You were only allowed clothing issued to you. If you-- if you were caught with something extra, the-- the penalty was so great, it wasn't worth taking the chance. So I figured I'm going to throw it to my father, once they go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I could see him go-- [? they ?] [? went ?] [? to find ?] a group was approaching. I tried to throw the sweater across the electric barbed wires. The guard noticed me, and he took a shot at me. He missed me. So I ran behind it-- the-- the-- the barrack, the corner of the barrack, so he doesn't see me again. The group was led away, and that's the last I saw of my father. I found out later on they were taken to a place called Stutthof, which was on the Baltic coast. It was another concentration camp, and from which he never came out. I continued to [INAUDIBLE] at Birkenau, doing different jobs. And then I was lucky enough to become a pupil to a Kapo. A Kapo was a foreman. This fellow happened to be an Austrian German. His name was Hans. I didn't know his other name. And his Kommando was building roads, so I was attending to his personal needs, as his pupil. I made sure his boots are polished, that his clothing is clean, and I was ready for any errands. Whatever he said, I was ready to-- to obey. And this afforded me extra food sometimes. And I could get extra clothing-- not extra, but different clothing-- like, say, instead wearing tattered pants, he would make sure I got a better pair of pants or a shirt or whatever. And we used to build roads. So it was evolved already. Building roads involved surveyors. The surveyors were Germans, not prisoners. They come out from outside. They used to lay out the roads, and we used to build them. It also involved going to the railway, to the same railway siding where the prisoners used to come in. They used to bring in the materials for building roads or taking out the clothings and the gold and the jewelry to Germany. So you had access to the railway line, in a group, of course, under a Kapo, under an overseer. One day, the people from Lodz came, in July, August. I think it was August. People from Lodz used to come. They used to disembark from the train. As soon as they disembarked from the train, we were going out in a group to collect stones for building the roads. This was a guise, actually. My job was to jump on the wagon before they were cleaned up, rummage through, and see if I can find anything that the Kapo wanted-- wanted-- conserves, like canned goods.

speaker: interviewer
text: For himself personally or-- or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave it to him. Whatever he did, I don't know. Conserves, salamis, which they brought from Lodz, and valuables-- money, gold. One day, I went on a-- on-- on-- on a-- on an expedition like this, and I found a wire full of wedding bands. It must have been about this big, full of wedding bands. They looked like gold to me. I figured, well, I'll give it to him. Anyway, I went back, and he was quite happy what I brought him back. He sold this ring-- this-- this-- this-- this container of wedding bands for two bottles of vodka to borrow these German surveyors. And a bottle of vodka between him and the [GERMAN], the secretary of the-- of the work gang-- they must have drank it in a couple of days. And the other bottle of vodka, he took me, and he taped it to my thigh. And of course, when we went to work there, we had to go outside of our camp on the German guard-- on the SS guard, of course, to the-- crossing the road, crossing the railway line, going into the [GERMAN] concentration camp, and there we were building the road. And from there, we used to send us out to the ramp to organize or to steal these-- these things. And as luck had it, on the way back to camp-- you know, when-- when you-- when you went out of the camp, orchestra plays [INAUDIBLE] martial music, and you had to march like a soldier in and march back. And so they searched us occasionally. And they found this bottle of vodka on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was taken to the guard house, and they played with me, the SS guard. He says, oh, you [GERMAN], you know, little boy-- he says, where did you get this bottle of vodka? And I said, I found it. They start pushing me around, and they start beating me up. Where did you get this bottle of vodka? I said, I found it. And I stuck to my-- to my story, I found it. And they beat me to a pulp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew if I told them that the Kapo, gave it to me, he's a German. They might demote him, but he will kill me. So that's out of the question. They beat me up so badly that when I woke up, I found myself in some strange barrack. Wasn't able to go to work. Lucky, he sent food to me. He made sure that I'm OK, more or less. I don't know. I didn't expect anything, but he didn't go near me. And it took me, I don't know, about a week or so. I recovered enough. I still have marks on my back from that beating that they gave me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Luckily, I recovered from this-- from this beating. So this was-- this is the story of my pupil days to this Kapo. He gave me a nice pair of officer's shoes. Maybe it was his, I don't know. They were too long, and I had to cut them down a little bit. And I remember marching out of Auschwitz with these boots, and they were a life-saver. But he was a bastard, that guy, to other prisoners. He used to beat people up for no reason at all, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no reason at all. And there were-- on Sundays, sometimes we didn't work. And they had what looked like a swimming pool in every camp. Actually, it was a hole, like a-- a cone, built like a cone, inverted like this, on-- on four sides. And it was full of water. This usually was the front of the kitchens of the camp. And they kept water in it in case there's a fire. So they used to have a reservoir of water. On Sunday, the Kapos, particularly the criminals-- most of the Kapos were criminal prisoners taken out from German jails and given the assignments to be Kapos or overseers or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trustees in the-- in the concentration camps. They used to push people in and don't let them out until they drowned. And this is what they-- this was their-- their sport on a Sunday. Occasionally, there were selections. Dr. Mengele was there. I came across him on a couple of occasions. God forbid you should show any-- any-- any-- any signs of having any skin peeling or any signs of being sick. He would pick you. At any time, transports used to arrive that didn't have enough people to fill the gas chambers. He would have enough people whose numbers he had to be called on to fill up the gas chambers. He measured you. If you weren't a meter and a half tall, he took your number down, and you're ready for the gas chambers right away. We used to watch transports arrive, just like when we arrived, except we arrived, there was no children, except I was one of the youngest ones. The people from Hungary, people from this-- from Lodz, people from-- from-- from-- from Czechoslovakia and Theresienstadt used to arrive there. They used to select enough people that they had orders for to fill some factory. They sent the people, 100 people, for some coal mine or for some factory or the people that died in camp to be filled up. The rest they used to march to the gas chambers, gas them, and burn them. And sometimes they used-- the transports used to arrive at a greater capacity than the gas-- than the gas chambers and the ovens were used to handle. So they had a big pit, looked like-- like a swimming pool. It used to burn day and night. They used to throw the children and the sick people in there alive. And this-- you get used to it because you expect that you're going to be next, and you didn't give it another thought. There was this Sonderkommando whose job it was to herd the people into the gas chambers and then pull them out after they'd been gassed to feed the-- feed the [INAUDIBLE] the crematoria. And a father of a friend of mine who I befriended in Birkenau-- nobody is supposed to know that this man had a son there. This wasn't a good thing to-- to-- to let any of the-- the administration know that you are related to anybody because they used you sometimes for these purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to come in for the Russians. They-- they were quartered in a-- in a camp. They had two barracks zoned apart from the-- from our camp. Nobody could go in or out except for this Sonderkommando except this Sonderkommando sometimes slept in the gas chambers, and they did this purposely. Any time they wanted to do away with them, [INAUDIBLE] they just dropped the gas in there, and the next day, they picked a new Sonderkommandos. So very few of the-- very, very few people survived. So they used to come and get the Russians to our-- to our camp. Of course, it was customary that you had no other means of obtaining food because they did, because people used to bring stuff. And they used to have enough food, better food, than the camp provided. So why they were coming for the Russians to our camp to collect them, they didn't need it so much. My friend, me, and another couple of boys used to go to their cart. They would turn their back and let us steal some of the bread. And we used to joke with his father. He says, make sure when you get there, you have some dry wood for us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is how life was valued there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- late in November or December, they picked us. They picked-- they used to pick-- they used to pick people to be sent away someplace, and we wanted to get out of there. So with any transport that you picked, you got away. They figured it wouldn't be any worse than here. But we could also see they are bringing in truckloads of people. They were called Mussulmans, like Muslims, skin-and-bone people. They used to bring them back to where they were being taken, say, a couple of months ago. They were still able-bodied to-- more or less able-bodied to work. They're bringing back skeletons.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we sometimes-- we could ask them where they're coming from. So they'd say they come from [NON-ENGLISH] or from this coal mine or from this mine. So in-- while we were in Birkenau and my father was taken away, there was a few boys. We made a kind of a clique. And we had different jobs, and we organized, like [? we ?] steal. And we shared it, and we helped each other. And we let the other prisoners know, don't bother us because we're going to gang up on you, and it worked. Otherwise, if you were susceptible, you were weak, other people would gang up on [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and this is how it was. We protected each other. And we were able to save cigarettes. This was the currency of the camp. For a cigarette, you could buy anything you wanted. So we had saved maybe six or eight cigarettes amongst us, and we-- and we kept them. And we heard that there was a transport going away, and we weren't invited. We were not picked. So I went and bribed the-- the-- the secretary there, and I said-- I said, I want these four boys and me to be included, and I gave him the cigarettes-- not all of the cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took out, I guess, four people or five people and included us. And then I found out that they're going to these coal mines from where these people are coming back. I says, oh, that's no good. They're going to bring us back, or they're going to die there, so I bribed him again. Out we come. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then another-- another time, they picked people to go away, so we-- we-- we tried to be included, and we were included. And they marched us out from Birkenau, just boys. We must have been around 14, 15 years old. They marched us to Auschwitz. That's where you see the sign Arbeit Macht Frei. That's the first time I've seen that because we were in Birkenau, which is three kilometers away. And we were made to wait in front of this gate there in front of the camp. They brought out some other boys from there, joined our group, and they marched us to a place called Hof Birkenau, which was a farm. It was all part of the Auschwitz complex. There were only one barracks of prisoners there. There were a barracks outside of a few guards, and of course, there were the overseers, the Kapos. And we worked on a farm there. We figured, by God, we're going to be work on a farm. It's going to be wonderful. All they had on the farm is horses and cows. And they had-- the crop were there was sugar beets. So we tried to eat the sugar beets. So a few guys got caught eating the sugar beets. It was cold winter already. If you were cold-- if you were caught eating the sugar beets, they would take you outside, stuck a sugar beet in your mouth, kick it in, kick all your teeth out, and leave you there on your knees in the wintertime. And you were bleeding like a pig. So you learned not to steal sugar beets anymore. But there was oats, and I used to come steal sometimes a handful of oats and eat it from the horses. Once I was caught, and luckily, this Kapo was an old German. He-- he seemed so ancient, and I've never seen an old person like that. Criminal prisoner, bastard, going around all day cursing. He caught me. And he said he's going to give me a caning. So I was ready. He says, go get yourself a cane. So I looked around in the barn there, and I made sure I got a heavy one so you don't feel the whips so much as you feel by a-- a-- a-- a thinner one. And he caned me. It wasn't so bad. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And we were there until January. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We could steal the odd handful of oats. No more sugar beets. And one day, there was a bombing. The airplanes came, Russian airplanes, and they bombed the camp. Outside the camp they had the anti-aircraft guns. I hid when the bombs came, when the-- when the-- when the airplanes came, and I hear a bomb drop. And I got myself-- my head up, and I see they leveled the SS barrack, right next to our barrack. Outside-- theirs was outside of the wires, ours was inside. But the SS men were in the bunkers. There was no one in the barrack there. Two days later, we were-- we were assembled together with the cattle and the horses, allowed to take a blanket. January, 1945. Marched out of the camp. We were marched out of the camp. When we hit the main road, you looked around. You would think the whole world is marching. We marched out with the cattle. Our guards rode the horses. We were marching. And we marched, and we marched, and we marched. And we marched for 100 miles. We marched from Auschwitz to the city of Breslau, which was in Silesia. And so many people died on that march that you couldn't march maybe for 100 meters not to see somebody dead on either side of the road. The guards, either they took a disliking to you, or you got slow, or you got just plainly gave up. So they shot you, and they got people on both sides of the road. We, our group of the five boys, we held onto each other, and we helped each other, and we marched all the way. One thing, you know, I-- I can't understand that until now-- I laugh about it, it's not funny thing, actually. We marched-- the snows were heavy. Sometimes we stopped at night, and they made us go and say-- whoever could go first into the barn-- they let the-- the horses get in there first. And whoever could sneak-- sneak in there, snuck in there, but the horses used to walk on you because everything was so crowded. A woman ran away, left the column. And she must have run maybe 100, 150 yards, and she couldn't run no more. The snow was up to her chest, and there she was stuck. We were marching. People, prisoners were being shot on both sides. Here the woman is yelling in agony. She cannot [INAUDIBLE] somebody shoot me, she says. She begged the guards to shoot her, and nobody shot her. They left her suffer just like this. I could still hear when we marched by, [? till a ?] please shoot me. And this is something that struck me, and I can't figure this out until today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe just the insanity of the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some things you just couldn't figure out. We marched to Breslau. And in Breslau, we get to the railway station there. And there was a train on the railway station. It was an open freight train. It was half full of snow, and we were made to board that train. They didn't even give us a chance to get the snow out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And off we went. And we went on an open train in January, as you can imagine. It was crowded. We had no food. There was no problem with water because you could always suck on the snow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how long we traveled. Three days, four days? I don't remember. It seemed like a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went into Germany. And when we reached Germany, we were in Buchenwald, which was near the city of Weimar. And I remember just like now-- getting off the train, I had to take my pants off because the pants were frozen to the wagon. Luckily, I had a piece of blanket that they let us take out of Birkenau. I wrapped myself, and we were admitted to Buchenwald, which was at that time overcrowded because they were evacuating all the camps in the east and marching them west. I guess that people marched to Bergen-Belsen, Buchenwald, Ravensbruck. All the-- all the camps were that way in Austria and in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The admittance to Buchenwald was a little bit similar to Auschwitz. You had to line up. They didn't give you a number because you had a number already. It was overcrowded. And we were assigned a camp that wasn't a barrack. It was-- I think it was a tent. And after a while, we were assigned a barrack. And of course, there was no work there. We didn't work. The overseers, the Kapos, were not as cruel as they were at Auschwitz because I understand they were either Russian prisoners of war, or they were political prisoners. At Auschwitz also they had a lot of-- in Buchenwald, like Auschwitz, they had a lot of prisoners that weren't Jewish. In-- in Auschwitz, the-- the-- the majority of prisoners weren't Jewish. A lot of Jews came in being gassed and burned, but actually, prisoners were not-- the majority were not Jewish. There were Russian prisoners of war, there were French, there were Yugoslavs, there were Belgians, there were-- you name it, and there they were. They were Germans, Austrians. Same thing in Buchenwald, but Buchenwald, I understand, was more prisoners of war and political prisoners. We were just thrown in there from the other camps. And there, since we had no work, you couldn't organize. You couldn't steal any food extra than what you were assigned as the-- as the rations. I remembered the-- the block [INAUDIBLE] where I was the longest time, his name was [? Gustav. ?] He was a Russian prisoner of war. I believe he was Jewish. I'm not sure, but nobody knew. And since we didn't work, except sometimes we used to volunteer to be taken down to the city of Weimar to clear the rubble so we have something to do, and we could get some extra food digging the cellars, like clearing the rubble. So we used to volunteer, and if you were lucky enough, you spend a day or two days in the week working in Weimar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since we didn't work besides this, I figured there's so many people being brought in from different camps, why don't I look around in the other barracks? Maybe I'll find my father. Maybe I'll find my brother. On this point, we'll stop for a moment.
